raw_text: REPORTABLE IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION CIVIL APPEAL Nos.823-854 OF 2016 (Arising out of SLP (C ) Nos. 15919- 15950 of 2011) Kerala Public Service Commission  Ors. ..Appellants Versus The State Information Commission  Anr. .Respondents With CIVIL APPEAL NO.855 OF 2016 (Arising out of SLP (Civil) No.5433 of 2014) Public Service Commission U.P. ..Appellant Versus Raghvendra Singh .. Respondent J U D G M E N T M.Y. EQBAL, J. Leave granted. 2. In these two appeals the short question which needs consideration is as to whether the Division Bench of the Kerala High Court by impugned judgment has right ly held that the respondents are entitled not only to get information with regard to the scan copies of their answer sheet, 1 561 Page 1 tabulation-sheet containing interview marks but also entitled to know the names of the examiners who have evaluate d the answer sheet. 3. The information sought for by the respondents were denied by the State Public Information Officer and the Appellate Authority. However, the State Information Commission allowed the second appeal and held that there is no fiduciary rela tionship in case of answer scripts. Further, the interview marks cannot be considered as personal information, since the public authority had already decided to publish them. 4. Both the High Courts of Kerala and Allahabad have taken the view, following th e earlier decisions of this Court that no fiduciary relationship exists between the appellants and the respondents and, therefore, the information sought for have to be supplied to them. 5. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and have gone through the impugned judgments passed by the 2 562 Page 2 Division Bench of the High Court of Kerala at Ernakulam and Allahabad. 6. So far as the information sought for by the respondents with regard to the supply of scanned copies of his answer -sheet of the written test, copy of the tabulation sheet and other information, we are of the opinion that the view taken in the impugned judgment with regard to the disclosure of these information, do not suffer from error of law and the same is fully justified. However, the view of the Kerala High Court is that the information seekers are also entitled to get the disclosure of names of examiners who have evaluated the answer-sheet. 7. The view taken by the Kerala High Court holding that no fiduciary relationship exists b etween the University and the Commission and the examiners appointed by them cannot be sustained in law. The Kerala High Court while observing held:- 16.What, if any , is the fiduciary relationship of the PSC qua the examinees? Performance audit of constitutional institutions would only 3 563 Page 3 strengthen the confidence of the citizenry in such institutions. The PSC is a constitutional institution. To stand above board, is one of its own prime requirements. There is nothing that should deter disclosure of the contents of the materials that the examinees provide as part of their performance in the competition for being selected to public service. The confidence that may be reposed by the examinees in the institution of the PSC does not inspire the acceptability of a fiduciary relationship that should kindle the exclusion of information in relation to the evalution or other details relating to the examination. Once the evaluation is over and results are declared, no more secrecy is called for. D issemination of such information would only add to the credibility of the PSC, in the constitutional conspectus in which it is placed. A particular examinee would therefore be entitled to access to information in relation to that persons answer scripts. A s regards others, information in relation to answer scripts may fall within the pale of third party information in terms of section 11 of the RTI Act. This only means that such information cannot be accessed except in conformity with the provisions conta ined in section 11. It does not, in any manner, provide for any immunity from access. 17. We shall now examine the next contention of PSC that there is a fiduciary relationship between it and the examiners and as a consequence, it is eligible to claim pro tection from disclosure, except with the sanction of the competent authority, as regards the identity of the examiners as also the materials that were subjected to the examination. We have already approved TREESA and the different precedents and commentari es relied on therein as regards the concept of fiduciary relationship. We are in full agreement with the law laid by the Division Bench of this Court in Centre of Earth Science 4 564 Page 4 Studies (supra), that S.8 (1)(e) deals with information available with the person in his fiduciary relationship with another; that information under this head is nothing but information in trust, which, but for the relationship would not have been conveyed or known to the person concerned. What is it that the PS C holds in trust for the examiners? Nothing. At the best, it could be pointed out that the identity of the examiners has to be insulated from public gaze, having regard to issues relatable to vulnerability and exposure to corruption if the identities of th e examiners are disclosed in advance. But, at any rate, such issues would go to oblivion after the conclusion of the evaluation of the answer scripts and the publication of the results. Therefore, it would not be in public interest to hold that there could be a continued secrecy even as regards the identity of the examiners. Access to such information, including as to the identity of the examiners, after the examination and evaluation process are over, cannot be shied off under any law or avowed principle of privacy. 8. We do not find any substance in the reasoning given by the Kerala High Court on the question of disclosure of names of the examiners. 9. In the present case, the PSC has taken upon itself in appointing the examiners to evaluate the answer papers and as such, the PSC and examiners stand in a principal -agent relationship. Here the PSC in the shoes of a Principal 5 565 Page 5 has entrusted the task of evaluating the answer papers to the Examiners. Consequently, Examiners in the positio n of agents are bound to evaluate the answer papers as per the instructions given by the PSC. As a result, a fiduciary relationship is established between the PSC and the Examiners. Therefore, any information shared between them is not liable to be disclos ed. Furthermore, the information seeker has no role to play in this and we dont see any logical reason as to how this will benefit him or the public at large. We would like to point out that the disclosure of the identity of Examiners is in the least inte rest of the general public and also any attempt to reveal the examiners identity will give rise to dire consequences. Therefore, in our considered opinion revealing examiners identity will only lead to confusion and public unrest. Hence, we are not inclined to agree with the decision of the Kerala High Court with respect to the second question. 6 566 Page 6 10. In the present case the request of the information seeker about the information of his answer sheets and details of the interview marks can be and should be provided to him. It is not something which a public authority keeps it under a fiduciary capacity. Even disclosing the marks and the answer sheets to the candidates will ensure that the candidates have been given marks according to their performance in the exam. This practice will ensure a fair play in this competitive environment, where candidate puts his time in preparing for the competitive exams, but, the request of the information seeker about the details of the person who had examinedchecked the paper cannot and shall not be provided to the information seeker as the relationship between the public authority i.e. Service Commission and the Examiners is totally within fiduciary relationship. The Commission has reposed trust on t he examiners that they will check the exam papers with utmost care, honesty and impartially and, similarly, the Examiners have faith that 7 567 Page 7 they will not be facing any unfortunate consequences for doing their job properly. If we allo w disclosing name of the examiners in every exam, the unsuccessful candidates may try to take revenge from the examiners for doing their job properly. This may, further, create a situation where the potential candidates in the next similar exam, especially in the same state or in the same level will try to contact the disclosed examiners for any potential gain by illegal means in the potential exam. 11. We, therefore, allow these appeals in part and modify the judgment only to the extent that the respondents -applicants are not entitled to the disclosure of names of the examiners as sought for by them. .J. (M.Y. Eqbal) .J. (Arun Mishra) New Delhi February 4, 2016 8 568 Page 8
facts: The case involves two appeals, one from the Kerala Public Service Commission and another from the Public Service Commission of Uttar Pradesh, regarding the disclosure of information under the Right to Information (RTI) Act. The respondents had sought information regarding their answer scripts, tabulation sheets, and the names of examiners who evaluated their answer sheets. The State Public Information Officer and the Appellate Authority denied the information, but the State Information Commission allowed the second appeal, holding that there is no fiduciary relationship in case of answer scripts.
issues: The main legal issues presented are: whether the respondents are entitled to get information regarding their answer scripts, tabulation sheets, and interview marks; whether the names of examiners who evaluated the answer sheets can be disclosed; and whether a fiduciary relationship exists between the public service commissions and the examiners appointed by them.
holding: The court held that the respondents are entitled to get information regarding their answer scripts, tabulation sheets, and interview marks, as the view taken by the High Courts of Kerala and Allahabad does not suffer from error of law. However, the court disagreed with the Kerala High Court's view that the names of examiners who evaluated the answer sheets can be disclosed, stating that a fiduciary relationship exists between the university and the commission and the examiners appointed by them.

debate_transcript.speaker: Plaintiff Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
Honorable Court, I stand before you today to advocate for the rights of the petitioners, who have been denied access to crucial information regarding their answer scripts, tabulation sheets, and interview marks by the Kerala Public Service Commission and the Public Service Commission of Uttar Pradesh. The Right to Information Act, 2005, is a powerful tool that empowers citizens to seek information from public authorities, promoting transparency and accountability.

In this case, the petitioners have sought information that is essential to understand the evaluation process and to assess the fairness of the examination. The denial of this information by the respondent commissions is a clear violation of the petitioners' right to information, guaranteed under Article 19(1)(a) of the Constitution of India. The Supreme Court has held in the case of S.P. Gupta vs. Union of India (1981) that the right to information is an integral part of the freedom of speech and expression.

The commissions' argument that a fiduciary relationship exists between them and the examiners is misconceived. The relationship between the commissions and the examiners is that of a principal and an agent, and not a fiduciary one. The examiners are appointed to evaluate answer scripts, and their role is to provide an objective assessment, not to act in a fiduciary capacity. I will demonstrate, through relevant legal precedents and provisions of the RTI Act, that the petitioners are entitled to the information they have sought, and that the respondent commissions have failed to justify their denial of this information.

debate_transcript.speaker: Defendant Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
My Lord, I strongly argue that the respondents are not entitled to disclose the names of examiners who evaluated their answer sheets. The court's holding that a fiduciary relationship exists between the university and the commission and the examiners appointed by them is well-founded. As per Section 8(1)(e) of the RTI Act, information available to a person in his fiduciary relationship is exempt from disclosure. 

The Supreme Court in the case of Gujarat Public Service Commission vs. R.M. Patel (2015) has held that the relationship between the examiners and the public service commission is fiduciary in nature. Disclosing the names of examiners would compromise their anonymity and independence, potentially influencing their evaluations and undermining the integrity of the examination process. 

Furthermore, I challenge the respondents' claim that they are entitled to get information regarding their answer scripts, tabulation sheets, and interview marks. While the RTI Act aims to promote transparency, it is not absolute. The exemption under Section 8(1)(e) applies in this case, as the information sought is related to the evaluation process, which is a fiduciary function. I urge the court to consider the potential consequences of disclosing such information and the need to maintain the confidentiality of the examination process. The defendants have acted in accordance with the law, and their decision to deny the information should be upheld.

debate_transcript.speaker: Plaintiff Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
Honorable members of the court, I stand before you today to advocate for the rights of the respondents, who have been denied access to crucial information regarding their answer scripts, tabulation sheets, and interview marks. The Right to Information Act, 2005, is a landmark legislation that empowers citizens to seek information from public authorities, promoting transparency and accountability. In this case, the respondents have sought information that is essential to understand the evaluation process and to verify the correctness of their marks.

The court's decision to allow the disclosure of answer scripts, tabulation sheets, and interview marks is a significant step towards upholding the principles of transparency and fairness. As held in the case of CBSE vs. Aditya Bandopadhyay (2011), the Supreme Court has established that evaluated answer scripts are not exempt from disclosure under the RTI Act. I argue that this precedent applies to the present case, and the respondents are entitled to access their evaluated answer scripts.

However, I strongly disagree with the court's decision to withhold the names of examiners who evaluated the answer sheets, citing a fiduciary relationship. I will demonstrate that this relationship does not exist in the context of public examinations, and the disclosure of examiners' names is necessary to ensure accountability and fairness in the evaluation process. I will cite relevant legal precedents, including the case of Girish Ramchandra Deshpande vs. Central Information Commission (2013), to support my argument.

debate_transcript.speaker: Defendant Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
Your Honor, I strongly argue that the respondents are not entitled to the information they seek. The Right to Information Act is not a tool for contestants to scrutinize the evaluation process, but rather to promote transparency in governance. The evaluation of answer scripts is a subjective process, and disclosing the names of examiners would compromise their independence and anonymity, which is essential for fair evaluation.

The court's decision to allow disclosure of answer scripts, tabulation sheets, and interview marks may lead to undue pressure on examiners and compromise the integrity of the examination process. I cite the judgment of the Supreme Court in the case of Maharashtra State Board of Secondary and Higher Secondary Education vs. Paritosh Bhupesh Kumar Sheth (2011), which held that the right to information is not absolute and may be restricted in cases where it may cause harm to the public interest.

Furthermore, I question the credibility of the respondents' claims, as they have not demonstrated any malpractice or bias in the evaluation process. The onus lies on them to prove that the evaluation was flawed, which they have failed to do. I urge the court to consider the potential consequences of allowing such disclosures and the impact it may have on the fairness and integrity of public service examinations. The defendant's position is that the information sought is exempt under Section 8(1)(j) of the RTI Act, as it relates to personal information of the examiners, and its disclosure would cause unwarranted invasion of their privacy.

reasoning: The Right to Information Act, 2005, aims to promote transparency and accountability in public authorities, and the petitioners have sought information that is essential to understand the evaluation process and to assess the fairness of the examination.
The Supreme Court's decision in CBSE vs. Aditya Bandopadhyay (2011) establishes that evaluated answer scripts are not exempt from disclosure under the RTI Act, which applies to the present case.

The court's holding that a fiduciary relationship exists between the university and the commission and the examiners appointed by them is limited to the disclosure of examiners' names, and does not extend to the disclosure of answer scripts, tabulation sheets, and interview marks.
supporting_evidence: S.P. Gupta vs. Union of India (1981)
Girish Ramchandra Deshpande vs. Central Information Commission (2013)

recommendations: The court should consider providing clear guidelines on the disclosure of examiners' names to maintain consistency in similar cases.
The RTI Act should be amended to explicitly address the disclosure of evaluated answer scripts and other examination-related information to avoid confusion and inconsistencies.
audit.summary: The verdict appears to be fair and unbiased, with the judge's reasoning based on the Right to Information Act and relevant case law. However, the decision could be improved by providing clearer guidelines on the disclosure of examiners' names and considering amendments to the RTI Act to address similar cases in the future. No discriminatory language or regional/caste/religion proxies were detected.
